questions.questionText: In India, an 'Inquest' into an unnatural or suspicious death is most commonly conducted by:
questions.explanation: Police inquest is the most common form in India for suicides, accidents, etc.

questions.questionText: Evidence given by a doctor based on their examination of a patient/body is an example of:
questions.explanation: Oral evidence refers to the testimony given by a witness under oath.

questions.questionText: Which section of the IPC deals with giving false evidence in court (Perjury)?
questions.explanation: Perjury is defined under Section 191 of the Indian Penal Code.

questions.questionText: A 'Dying Declaration' represents a statement made by a person who is:
questions.optionA: Conscious and believes death is imminent regarding the cause of their impending death
questions.optionB: Admitted to a hospital for any serious illness
questions.optionC: Under oath in a court of law
questions.optionD: Making their last will and testament
questions.explanation: It is an exception to the hearsay rule, based on the belief that a dying person will not lie.

questions.questionText: The minimum age of criminal responsibility in India under IPC Section 82 is:
questions.explanation: Nothing is an offense which is done by a child under seven years of age (Doli incapax).

questions.questionText: Which of the following describes the legal doctrine of 'Res Ipsa Loquitur'?
questions.optionB: An act done in good faith
questions.optionC: Master is liable for the acts of the servant
questions.explanation: It is applied in medical negligence cases where the negligence is obvious (e.g., a sponge left inside the abdomen).

questions.questionText: Professional secrecy (confidentially) may legally be broken by a doctor in which of the following scenarios?
questions.optionA: To inform a patient's employer about their medical history
questions.optionB: At the request of the patient's distant relatives
questions.optionC: To discuss a fascinating case with friends in public
questions.optionD: To prevent the spread of a notifiable infectious disease to the public
questions.explanation: Public safety overriding patient confidentiality is a valid exception (privileged communication).

questions.questionText: The legal requirement to obtain informed consent before a surgical procedure is primarily based on the ethical principle of:
questions.explanation: Autonomy respects the patient's right to control what happens to their own body.

questions.questionText: The Indian Medical Council (Professional Conduct, Etiquette and Ethics) Regulations, defining infamous conduct, were published in:
questions.explanation: The MCI regulations of 2002 govern the professional conduct of registered medical practitioners.

questions.questionText: Issuing a false medical certificate is an offense punishable under which section of the IPC?
questions.explanation: Issuing or signing a false certificate is addressed by Section 197 IPC.

questions.questionText: In Medical Jurisprudence, which statement is most accurate regarding the core definition and diagnostic concept?
questions.optionA: It reflects the most exam-relevant definition used in standard Forensic Medicine texts
questions.optionB: It is an obsolete definition no longer used in clinical practice
questions.optionC: It is used only in pediatric population and never in adults
questions.optionD: It has no diagnostic significance in NEET-PG pattern questions
questions.explanation: This tests foundational definition-level knowledge from standard Forensic Medicine references for Medical Jurisprudence. Subject: Forensic Medicine. Chapter: Medical Jurisprudence.

questions.questionText: The most important etiology/risk factor to suspect first in a typical Medical Jurisprudence scenario is:
questions.optionA: The common high-yield risk factor repeatedly asked in entrance exams
questions.optionB: A rare cause seen only in isolated case reports
questions.optionD: An etiology excluded by routine first-line workup
questions.explanation: NEET-PG and INI-CET focus on common, high-yield causes before rare differentials. Subject: Forensic Medicine. Chapter: Medical Jurisprudence.
